title: Chapter 25: The New Pact
content: I didn't knock.

The chairman's office still smelled like Robert — old paper and cedar and the particular must of a man who treated a room like a vault. Three years since he'd been dragged out of it, and the scent clung to the walls like a ghost that refused to vacate. But the figure behind the desk was different. Darker. Leaner. The sharp line of his jaw backlit by the green-glass banker's lamp that Robert had once used to intimidate subordinates.

Heath didn't look up when I entered. His eyes moved across the document in front of him — a financial statement, columns of numbers that would have bored anyone else to catatonia. His pen traced a line. A small annotation in the margin.
"I'm busy."

I sat down across from him. The leather chair groaned — the same chair Robert had sat in when he'd explained to me, with all the warmth of a mortician, that my family's debts were now Blackwood's assets. I placed my notebook on the desk between us.
It landed with a weight that had nothing to do with its physical mass.

Heath's eyes flicked to it. The black cover. The frayed elastic band holding it shut. The spine cracked from three years of being opened and closed and opened again. He knew what it was. He'd seen me writing in it — in the conservatory, in the library, in the dark of his dorm room while he pretended to sleep. He'd never asked what I was recording. That had been part of our old arrangement: he didn't ask, I didn't explain, and the distance between us was called loyalty.
I pulled off the elastic band.

"I have what you need." I opened the notebook to the first page — a spiderweb of names and connections, dates and amounts, drawn in three colors of ink. "And you have what I need. So let's stop pretending we're not going to do this."

Heath set down his pen. Slowly. Deliberately. The way someone sets down a weapon when they're deciding whether to pick up a different one. He leaned back in Robert's chair — his chair now, though he never seemed comfortable in it — and studied me.

The lamp light carved him into geometry: the hollows beneath his cheekbones, the angle of his brow, the way his dark hair fell across his forehead like it was trying to hide something. He looked tired. Not the soft tired of lost sleep — the worn-down tired of a man who'd been grinding against stone for three years and was finally starting to feel the stone win.

But his eyes were awake. Those were always the danger. The rest of Heath Cruz could look exhausted, dismantled, barely holding together, and his eyes would still be calculating every variable in the room.
"Why now?" he asked. Not curious. Testing. "You had three years to bring this to me."

"Because three years ago I was trying to protect you by keeping you out." I didn't look away from him. That was new too — the old Cat would have looked away, would have offered the truth sideways like a coin slipped across a table. This Cat put it down in the center and let it sit. "Now I know the only way to protect you is to put you in the center of it."

The silence that followed had a texture. Rougher than the old silences between us. The ones we'd filled with half-truths and strategic omissions and the carefully maintained fiction that what we felt wasn't relevant to what we did.
"That sounds like love," Heath said.

"It sounds like strategy." My voice didn't waver. I'd practiced this — not the words, but the tone. The removal of every soft thing from my throat. "If it's also love, that's a data point, not a decision."
Something moved behind his eyes. Not surprise — Heath was never surprised by the things people admitted. More like recognition. The way you look at a mirror and finally see yourself clearly.
He reached into the drawer of the desk and pulled out a folder. Thick. Bound with a clip that had the Cruz Holdings logo debossed into the metal. He set it beside my notebook.

"Debt structure," he said. "Every note Blackwood holds. Every covenant, every call provision, every pressure point where money meets power." He opened the folder. Pages of financial architecture — spreadsheets, term sheets, org charts that traced ownership like blood vessels. "I've been building this since the day I took this chair. Three board seats held by proxies who answer to Cruz capital. Two more where the directors have personal debts I acquired at discount. The financial leverage to collapse Blackwood's endowment inside a quarter."
He said it the way someone else might describe the weather. Flat. Factual. But I could see the strain in his knuckles where he gripped the folder's edge.
I turned the pages of my notebook.

"The Linton trust," I said. "It's the foundation of the board's voting bloc. But the trust instrument has a survivorship clause that was never properly amended after Serena Linton's death. The right challenge voids her seat — and the seat controls three proxy votes." I flipped forward. "Councilman Birch's daughter is in a private psychiatric facility in Vermont. Paid for through a shell company that traces back to Blackwood's discretionary fund. He votes however Robert tells him to because he can't afford the exposure." Another page. "Robert's offshore accounts. Three of them. Caymans, Malta, and one in the Seychelles that even Robert probably forgot about. I have routing numbers, transaction records, and a forensic accountant in Boston who'll testify if we need her to."
Heath was watching my hands as I turned the pages. Not my face. My hands. Like he was reading the tremor in my fingers — there wasn't one, but he was checking anyway.
"Together," he said slowly, "we have the complete blueprint."

"Not just the blueprint." I turned to the last section of the notebook — the section I'd started six months ago, when I'd finally accepted that the only way through this was through it. "The sequence. What to hit first, what follows, what falls on its own. Dependencies. Timelines. Contingencies if any single piece fails."
I looked up at him.
Heath's jaw tightened. Not anger — something more complicated. The expression of a man who'd been fighting alone in a room and suddenly discovered there was a door.
"We do this together," he said. Not a question. A term. "No more solo operations. No more protecting each other by lying."
"Agreed. Full transparency."
"Including about us."

The word *us* landed between us like a lit match. I felt the heat of it in my sternum, spreading outward through my ribs. The old Cat would have deflected. Would have redefined the terms, shifted the frame, made *us* mean something strategic and therefore safe.
I hesitated. The hesitation cost me — I could see him clocking it, measuring the distance between my instinct and my answer. But I made myself say it anyway.
"Including about us."
The room felt different after that. Less like an office and more like a space where something was about to be built — or demolished. The difference, with us, had never been clear.

Heath extended his hand across the desk. Not for a handshake — palm up. The pact pose. The gesture I'd taught him on the night we first decided that the two of us, together, were more dangerous than any number of enemies.

I looked at his palm. The scar sat just below the base of his fingers — a pale line, slightly raised, where three years ago we'd pressed our bleeding hands together in the conservatory and sworn something we didn't fully understand. I had the same scar on my own palm. I'd traced it often enough to know its topography by heart.
I placed my palm against his.

The pressure was immediate — warm skin, the slight damp of a hand that had been gripping a pen for hours, the ridge of scar against scar aligning with a precision that felt planned. Like our bodies had been waiting for this alignment, this registration, this key turning in a lock.
Neither of us bled this time.
"Same terms?" he asked. His voice was lower. Not soft — Heath's voice was never soft — but close. Like he was speaking from the inside of something.
"New terms," I corrected. "Last time I was the mind and you were the hand. This time we're both both."

Heath closed his hand around mine. Not gentle. Not rough. Equal. The pressure of his grip was exactly the pressure of mine — I could feel it, the way our strengths met and held without either side yielding or dominating. His scar pressed harder into mine. A reminder, not a wound.
"New terms," he repeated.
We let go. But the warmth stayed on my palm like a brand.
Then we got to work.

I spread my notebook open across the desk, the pages fanning out in their careful sequence — red ink for targets, blue for methods, black for timelines. Heath pulled the documents from his folder and laid them alongside, the financial architecture meeting the intelligence architecture like two halves of a map that had been waiting to be joined.

The desk became a battlefield. Papers overlapped, edges meeting at angles, annotations pointing to annotations in a web that only the two of us could read. Our shoulders were almost touching — I could feel the heat of him through the sleeve of his shirt, could smell the faint cedar of the office mixing with something sharper, something that was just him. Black coffee and old paper and the metallic edge of a man who hadn't left this room in hours.
"That trust challenge," Heath said, reaching across me to tap the Linton section. "If we file before the quarterly meeting—"
"We lose the element of surprise. File during. Let Birch see the challenge land in real time. He'll fold before Robert can countermand."
"By then Robert's already in the wind."
"No. Robert's already three moves behind. He doesn't know you have the offshore routing numbers. He thinks Malta is clean."

Heath's hand was still stretched across me, his index finger resting on the notebook page. He pulled it back slowly, and his knuckle grazed my shoulder. Neither of us acknowledged it. We hadn't needed to acknowledge physical proximity in years — our bodies had their own choreography, old as the first time we'd stood side by side in this hellhouse and decided to be each other's armor.

I reached for the same page he was pointing to. Our hands arrived together. His index finger and mine landed on the same line of text — *Birch, psychiatric facility, Vermont* — and for a half-second our touch lingered. Not intentional. Not accidental either.
"District court," I said. "Northern jurisdiction. Judge Morahan owes me a favor from the Harlow case."
"Of course she does." The faintest shift in his mouth. Not a smile. Heath Cruz didn't smile at things like this — he *acknowledged* them, the way a wolf acknowledges a clean kill. "You've been busy."
"You've been richer. We all have our talents."

The hours bled through the windows. The green-glass lamp threw our shadows across the papers — two shapes bending over a shared purpose, the borders between them impossible to determine. We spoke in half-sentences. *What if Whitmore balks?* — *He won't, the pension exposure* — *Right, but the timing has to* — *Weekend filing, let the news cycle* — *Before the board convenes, yes.* The conversation was a machine we'd built together and then abandoned, and now we were powering it up again and it ran smoother than either of us remembered.

At some point Heath shifted in his chair and his knee pressed against mine under the desk. He didn't move it. I didn't move it. The contact was steady, grounding — a point of warmth in the cold mathematics of destruction we were calculating.
We were mapping how to burn Blackwood Academy to the ground.
And it was the most intimate thing we'd done since the conservatory.

I felt it then — something releasing in my chest, a tension I'd been carrying so long I'd forgotten it wasn't part of my skeleton. The feeling of being *myself*. Not the half-version who'd limped through three years of solitary war, orchestrating at a distance, never trusting anyone with the full picture. Not the girl who'd wielded Heath like a weapon and called it protection. The real version. The one who had an equal, finally, and who trusted that equal enough to show him everything.

The paper smell was thick in the air — old documents and fresh ink and the vanilla note of aged bond paper. My palm still held the ghost-pressure of his grip. And outside, the sky was shifting from black to gray, the first pale wash of dawn bleeding through the tall windows.

Heath stood. The chair rolled back. He stretched — arms above his head, spine arching, the hem of his shirt lifting to reveal the sharp line of his hip and the dark edge of a scar I didn't recognize. New. Something from the years I'd missed.

He looked at me across the desk. Papers everywhere. Our combined intelligence laid out like the entrails of something we were about to sacrifice. The lamp light caught the fatigue in his face and the clarity in his eyes, and both were absolute.
"This is going to destroy everything," he said.
"Good." I gathered the papers into a neat stack — not because I wanted them ordered, but because my hands needed something to do. "That's the point."
"Including what's left of us."
I met his eyes. The dawn light was catching them now — gray-green and steady, not wavering, not blinking. Waiting for my answer the way he waited for everything: completely.
"If it destroys us, we weren't strong enough." I held his gaze. "And we've never been the type to settle for not-strong-enough."
Heath almost smiled.
Almost.

The dawn kept coming through the windows, stretching across the floor, touching the edges of our conspiracy. Somewhere in this building, Blackwood's board members were sleeping, and Robert was sleeping, and the institution they'd built on secrets and debt and leverage was sleeping too.
They didn't know yet that two predators had finally remembered that they hunted best together.
We were going to wake them up.
